1:
The whole cloud of witnesses reads this opening scene the same way: scribes and Pharisees arrive from Jerusalem not to learn but to accuse, and the very timing condemns them. The Antiochene and grammatical-historical voices, with one mind, fasten on the word "then." Chrysostom (twice) and the modern expositors (Barnes, Robertson) note that they came precisely when Christ had wrought "countless miracles" and healed by the touch of his hem — so their cavil over handwashing exposes a malice "repressed by nothing." Robertson adds that Jerusalem is "the headquarters of the conspiracy," already plotting with the Herodians (Mark 3:6; Matthew 12:14); the unusual order "Pharisees and scribes" shows the capital's guardians of tradition co-operating with the provincials.

The Latin fathers of the Catena press the contrast between the law of God and the inventions of men. Augustine insists that Christ here shows the law to be God's, while the Jews had "traditions foreign to the prophetical and canonical books"; Jerome marvels that they accuse the Son of God of breaking "the commandments of men," when true washing is of the mind, not the hands. Remigius notes their double fault — coming from the holy city, and as teachers who came "not to learn but to reprove." Bede traces the error to a carnal reading of "Wash, and be ye clean" (Isaiah 1:16), and the Gloss unfolds the corban evasion that follows (vv.4-6).

The Reformed reading (Calvin, Gill) lifts the episode into a permanent warning about worship. Calvin calls the passage "particularly worthy of our attention," because men display an "extraordinary insolence" in "perpetually contriving new modes of worshipping God"; once human authority assumes the right to command, tyranny follows, "and the worship of God is corrupted, of which the first and leading principle is obedience." Matthew Henry (puritan-evangelical) sharpens the irony that these "great men" of the holy city, who "should therefore have been better than others," were worse — "Jerusalem, which should have been a pure spring, was now become a poisoned sink."

Where the voices differ is only in accent. The fathers debate the precise force of the corban formula; the grammarians stress the historical conspiracy; the Reformed make it a mirror for every age that prefers its own ceremonies to God's command. Wesley simply points to the parallel in Mark 7:1. None dissents from the central charge: a religion zealous for its own rules and careless of God's is hypocrisy waiting to be unmasked (vv.7-9).
2:
On the specific charge — "Why do thy disciples transgress the tradition of the elders? for they wash not their hands when they eat bread" — the witnesses agree that the issue is ceremonial defilement, not hygiene, and that the elders' tradition had been exalted above Scripture itself. The grammatical-historical voices supply the documentation. Barnes and Robertson explain the rabbinic theory of a second, oral law delivered to Moses on Sinai and handed down "from father to son," now codified in the Mishnah and Talmuds; this washing "is not a requirement of the Old Testament," yet the rabbis "made it a mark of righteousness." Ellicott, following Mark 7:3-4, clarifies that what they feared "was not cleanliness as such, but the avoidance of ceremonial pollution" — contact with a heathen or a publican in the market — so the act of the disciples ranked, in their casuistry, with sexual impurity and exposed them to excommunication.

Lightfoot and Clarke pile up the rabbinic citations that show the staggering value placed on these traditions: "The words of the scribes are lovely above the words of the law"; "The words of the elders are weightier than the words of the prophets." Clarke recounts the legendary chain of transmission from Moses through Joshua, the prophets, and the men of the great synagogue down to Hillel — the system by which, our Lord could say, "Ye have made the word of God of no effect by your traditions." Both note the superstition of the demon "Shibta," supposed to sit on unwashed food.

The Reformed voice (Calvin) draws the careful distinction the rest assume: political laws are not in view, nor every human tradition, but those "imagined to be necessary to the worship of God," which thereby "lay a snare for the conscience." The washing itself "could not in itself be charged with wicked superstition" — Christ permitted the water-pots at Cana (John 2:6) — "but the fault lay in this, that they did not think that God could be properly worshipped in any other way." Gill adds that they preferred these unwritten rules even to the law of God. Wesley simply glosses "the elders" as "the chief doctors or teachers among the Jews." The schools thus converge: a tradition treated as binding on conscience, where God has not bound it, is the seed of the whole error Christ is about to expose.
3:
Christ answers a question with a question, and the witnesses note the strategy as much as the substance. The grammatical-historical commentators read it as a deliberate counter-charge. Barnes observes that the disciples were accused "as though [the traditions] were obligatory," and Christ "implied that they were not bound to obey their traditions," since these "violated the commandments of God." Ellicott calls the reply "an indirect one, an argumentum ad hominem": their casuistry stood "in direct opposition to the commandment of God," and the natural inference was that "apart from the commandment, it had no binding authority as a rule of life." Robertson presses the Greek dia tēn paradosin — "because of," not "by means of," your tradition — and frames the clash exactly: "Tradition is not good or bad in itself," but when it is placed "in the place of the commandment of God" there can be "no compromise," and Jesus "stands for real righteousness and spiritual freedom, not for bondage to mere ceremonialism."

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) puts the recrimination bluntly: "Ye accuse my disciples of transgressing the traditions of the elders — I accuse you of transgressing the commands of God," and that "in favor of your own tradition; thus preferring the inventions of men to the positive precepts of God."

The Reformed reading (Calvin, Gill) widens the lens to the principle. Calvin distinguishes Christ's two answers — one to the persons, one to the matter — and notes that here Christ "does not as yet pronounce a direct acquittal of his disciples," but exposes "how improper and unwarrantable is this readiness to take offense." Their wrath "is kindled rather by ambition than by a proper kind of zeal, when they thus prefer men to God." He famously turns the charge on the Papists of his own day, who "consider the act of eating a morsel of flesh as nothing less than a capital crime, while theft or fornication is regarded as a venial fault, and thus, on account of their traditions, they overturn the Law of God." Gill simply notes that Christ took no notice of "an human tradition, of no moment," and made "a very just recrimination." The whole cloud agrees: the burden of proof has been reversed, and the accusers now stand accused of the graver crime — setting human commandment above divine.
4:
Christ grounds the indictment in the Decalogue, and the witnesses trace his proof to two distinct commands. Origen (Alexandrian) is the careful textual critic here: Jesus accuses them "not with reference to a tradition of the Jewish elders, but with regard to two most imperative commandments of God" — the fifth, "Honour thy father and thy mother" (Exodus 20:12), and the sentence of Leviticus 20:9, "He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death." Origen notes that Matthew "has quoted them in part and in an abridged form," and even raises the puzzle whether the cursing-clause is "extraneous" to the corban case — a question he leaves open.

The grammatical-historical voices unfold the breadth of "honour." Clarke insists the word "was taken in great latitude of meaning among the Jews: it not only meant respect and submission, but also to take care of a person, to nourish and support him" (Numbers 22:17; 1 Timothy 5:17). Barnes likewise defines honouring as "to obey, to reverence... to speak kindly," and cursing as "to disobey, to treat with irreverence... to do any evil to a parent." Ellicott meets the obvious objection — that Christ himself bade men forsake father and mother for the gospel (Matthew 10:37) — and answers that "a lower, natural duty was to give way exceptionally to a higher and supernatural one; otherwise it remained in full force," whereas the Pharisees made the natural duty "give way to one which was purely human, arbitrary, and conventional."

Wesley reads "honour" as implying "all such relief as they stand in need of." Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern) supplies the catechetical balance: "while honouring our heavenly Father let us honour also the fathers of our flesh," for the Lord said not "He that loveth father or mother is not worthy of Me," but added "more than Me." Children must obey parents "in all things" — yet when earthly fathers "are of a contrary mind to our Father in heaven, then we must obey Christ's word." The schools agree: the fifth commandment binds with the sanction of death, and to honour parents means concretely to provide for them — exactly what the corban tradition (vv.5-6) was contrived to evade.
5:
Here the witnesses expound the corban device by which the tradition voided the command, and they are unanimous in its analysis even as they multiply the documentation. The grammatical-historical voices give the mechanics. Barnes explains corban as a thing "dedicated to the service of God, and therefore not to be appropriated to any other use"; if a son in anger declared his property corban, "the property could not be recalled, and the son was not under obligation to aid a parent with it." He draws the chilling parallel of the Sandwich Islands' tabu, by which "the chiefs and priests... had complete power over all the possessions of the people." Robertson adds that "by an angry oath of refusal to help one's parents, the oath or vow was binding," and "sometimes unfilial sons paid graft to the rabbinical legalists for such dodges." Lightfoot shows from the rabbinic codes that a son was bound "to provide his father meat and drink... yea, to beg for him" — so the tradition did not openly deny this duty, "but consequently by this tradition they made all void."

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) gives corban its sense — "an offering of approach; something consecrated to the service of God" — and turns it into a thundering rebuke of medieval almsgiving that left "the principal lands in the nation" in the hands of the Church while families starved: "It is sacrilege to dedicate that to God which is taken away from the necessities of our parents and children." Wesley reads it as "I have given, or at least purpose to give, to the treasury of the temple, what you might otherwise have had from me."

Ellicott traces the warped logic: "to divert to lower human uses that which has been consecrated to God is sacrilege," so once a man named his property corban he was "bound not to expend it on the support even of his nearest relations" — yet "the time of fulfilling the vow was left to his own discretion," leaving him the usufruct for life. Thus "the Corban practice became an easy method of evading natural obligations." Gill notes it was their own tradition that a man might so answer a poor parent. No school dissents: a vow framed as piety became a cloak for covetousness and cruelty.
6:
This short verse — "he shall be free... and ye have made the commandment of God of none effect by your tradition" — draws brief but pointed comment, and the witnesses are wholly grammatical-historical here. Ellicott corrects the text: the words "He shall be free" are not in the Greek and "are not wanted to complete the sense." The better reading runs straight on — "Whosoever shall say to his father or his mother, It is a gift... he shall not honour (i.e., shall not support) his father or his mother." Ellicott identifies "honour" with "the duty which was its first and most natural expression," and notes that the Pharisees "had inverted the right relation of the two, and made the tradition an end, and not a means." He points to Mark's "touch of grave and earnest irony" — "Full well ye reject the commandment of God, that ye may keep your own traditions" (Mark 7:9).

Robertson fastens on the verb: "Ye have made void the word of God" (ekyrōsate ton logon tou theou). The root "means force or authority," and the compound "is without authority, null and void" — "a late verb... in the LXX (Galatians 3:17) and in the papyri" used "in legal phraseology like cancelling a will." His verdict is sharp: "It was a stinging indictment that laid bare the hollow pretence of their quibbles about handwashing. The moral force of God's law is annulled by their hairsplitting technicalities and immoral conduct."

Barnes simply cross-references the sanction, "Honour not... Deuteronomy 27:16," letting the curse on him who dishonours his parent fall by implication on the system that taught such dishonour. The unanimous note is one of legal annulment: by their casuistry the scribes had not merely neglected the fifth commandment but cancelled it, as a court cancels a binding deed — and they had done so while posing as the law's most scrupulous defenders. The thin comment of the witnesses here is itself telling: the indictment needs no elaboration, only the exposure of a religion that uses devotion to God as a tool against the plain duty God commands.
7:
Christ turns from argument to prophecy, branding them hypocrites in the words of Isaiah 29:13, and the cloud of witnesses unfolds both the charge and the larger turn it sets in motion. The Antiochene Chrysostom marks the structural shift: having "added weight to His accusation of the Pharisees by the testimony of the Prophet, and not having amended them, He now ceases to speak to them, and turns to the multitudes." Christ frames his coming "high dogma" gently — "Hear and understand" — drawing his proof "from natural things." The Latin fathers define the term: Remigius says "Hypocrite signifies dissembler, one who feigns one thing in his outward act, and bears another thing in his heart," and the Jews are "well called hypocrites because under cover of God's honour they sought to heap up for themselves earthly gain." Raban hears Isaiah speaking "in the person of the Lord," foreseeing that they would "craftily oppose the Gospel." Jerome and Augustine, anticipating vv.10-11, defend the saying that what enters the mouth does not defile against the Manichaeans, with Augustine distinguishing the natural and the typical uncleanness of creatures.

The grammatical-historical voices stress the aptness and the reach of the citation. Barnes notes Isaiah "properly characterized the nation in calling them hypocrites," and "the words are applicable to the nation at all times." Robertson finds "sarcasm in this pointed application": Isaiah "beautifully pictured" them, and "the portrait was to the very life." Clarke pauses to defend the spelling "Isaiah," and adds the homiletic point that "when a hypocrite is found out, he should be exposed to all; this may lead to his salvation."

The Reformed Calvin distills the doctrine within the rebuke: the charge divides into two — "they relied on outward ceremonies alone, and set no value on true holiness," and "they worshipped God in a wrong way, according to their own fancy." Hence "the worship of God is spiritual, and does not consist in the sprinkling of water," and "there is no reasonable worship of God but what is directed by the rule of his word." Gill marshals even a rabbinic witness against the men of Jerusalem, and Wesley reads Isaiah's description of the fathers as "exactly applicable to you." All agree: hypocrisy is the heart-sin that lip-service to God only thinly conceals.
8:
"This people draweth nigh unto me with their mouth... but their heart is far from me." The witnesses, here almost wholly grammatical-historical and devotional, dwell on the gap between profession and heart that defines hypocrisy. Barnes states the principle plainly: "they are regular in the forms of worship... and keep the law outwardly; but God requires the heart, and that they have not rendered." Clarke makes it experiential: "The true worship of God consists in the union of the heart to him — where this exists not, a particle of the spirit of devotion cannot be found." He also notes a text-critical curiosity — that the opening clause "is omitted by several excellent MSS." and bracketed by Griesbach — yet refuses to drop it, since "I find it in the prophet, the place from which it is quoted."

Gill expands the outward show with sympathy for how convincing it looked: "They approached the ordinances of God, and drew nigh to him... they prayed unto him publicly, and constantly, in the streets, in the synagogues, and temple, and with much seeming devotion and sanctity." The point is not that their religion was scanty but that it was hollow — all surface, no centre.

Ellicott connects the citation backward to Matthew 13:14, where "the Pharisees were taught to see their own likeness in the language of the prophet"; now "the mirror is held up once more, and they are seen to have been anticipated in that very substitution of human for divine ordinances for which our Lord reproves them." Wesley draws the universal application that the witnesses all assume: "Their heart is far from me — and without this all outward worship is mere mockery of God." Matthew Henry, commenting on the larger passage (see v.1), had already named the two marks of a hypocrite — "in their own performances of religious worship" the heart is far off, and "in their prescriptions to others" they "teach for doctrines the commandments of men." The schools converge on a single diagnosis: God reads the heart, and where the heart is absent, the most diligent worship is mockery.
9:
"But in vain do they worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men." The witnesses read this closing line of Isaiah's oracle as the verdict on will-worship, and the Alexandrian Origen sets it in its fuller prophetic context: Christ, "wishing to refute completely from the words of the prophets all these traditions of the elders," brings forward the saying, which "Matthew has not written out... in the very words." Origen reaches back to the preceding verses of Isaiah about the spirit of stupor and the sealed book, finding the Pharisees' blindness foretold.

The grammatical-historical voices fasten on the two operative words. Barnes: "their attempts to worship are vain, or are not real worship — they are mere forms," and the "doctrines" are "things to be believed and practised in religion" which "God only has a right to declare." Ellicott refines the translation: the "doctrines" are "not articles of faith... but precepts which were taught as binding," and the "commandments" are "single, special rules as contrasted with the divine commandment, which was exceedingly broad." Gill notes that the Hebrew reads "their fear toward me," rightly rendered "worship," and that "in vain" is wanting in Isaiah — a textual point he weighs with Grotius and the Septuagint.

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) universalizes the rebuke beyond the Jews: "whole Christian Churches, as well as sects and parties, have acted in the same way. Men must not mould the worship of God according to their fancy"; however sincere, worship "of man's commandment... profits him nothing. Christ himself says it is in vain."

The Reformed Calvin makes this the locus classicus against ethelothrēskeia, "will-worship" (Colossians 2:23): "since God chooses to be worshipped in no other way than according to his own appointment, he cannot endure new modes of worship to be devised." The graver the labour spent on such inventions, "the heavier is the condemnation," for "obedience is better than sacrifice" (1 Samuel 15:22). Wesley adds the sting: to teach "the commandments of men as equal with, nay, superior to, those of God — what can be a more heinous sin?" The whole cloud agrees: worship not commanded by God is not merely deficient but vain, and the men who impose it stand under prophetic condemnation.
10:
Christ summons the crowd — "Hear, and understand" — and the witnesses see in the gesture itself a turning point. Ellicott calls the act "more startling and suggestive than appears on the surface": Jesus "removed the case... to another court, which His opponents did not recognise," turning "from the disputes and traditions of the schools to the unperverted conscience of the common people." Gill reads it as Christ leaving the obstinate scribes — "judging it not worth his while to say any more to men so obstinate" — and calling the humbler crowd, "knowing that they were more tractable, and teachable."

The Alexandrian Origen lifts the saying to its doctrinal height: the words teach that the Levitical food-laws are not exhausted "in any superficial understanding of them"; since Christ, "according to Mark, said these things making all meats clean," we "are not defiled when we eat those things which the Jews... declare to be unclean," but "are then defiled when... we speak offhand and discuss matters we ought not, from which there comes to us the spring of sins." The Pharisees, "offended at this saying," prove themselves "not the plant of his own Father in heaven" (anticipating v.13).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest exposition, and it carries the whole pericope. He notes the "solemn introduction" — Christ "turns from [the proud] as wilful and unteachable, and turns to the multitude, who, though weak, were humble, and willing to be taught." He states the truth in two propositions: "Not that which goes into the mouth defileth the man," for "the kingdom of God is not meat and drink" (Romans 14:17); and "that which comes out of the mouth, this defiles a man" — "we are polluted... by the words we speak from an unsanctified heart" (Ecclesiastes 5:6). Henry adds the irony that "those most defile themselves, who are most forward to censure the defilements of others," and warns that "if we please men with the concealment of truth... we are not the servants of Christ."

The Reformed Calvin agrees that "pollution does not come from without into a man, but that the fountain is concealed within him," and draws the pastoral lesson from Christ's refusal to soothe the offended: "we ought not to be exceedingly solicitous to please every one." Barnes and Clarke press the imperative — "make it a point of conscience to attend to the ministry of the word" — and see the great correction: the seat of defilement "is within. It is the heart itself."
11:
"Not that which goeth into the mouth defileth the man; but that which cometh out of the mouth, this defileth the man." The witnesses recognize a principle of vast reach, and the grammatical-historical voices measure how far it cuts. Ellicott calls it "a great broad principle, which not only cut at the root of Pharisaism, but, in its ultimate tendency, swept away the whole Levitical system of ceremonial purity — the distinction between clean and unclean meats." Yet he notes how slowly even the disciples grasped it: "Even after the day of Pentecost, Peter still prided himself on the observance of the Law... and boasted that he had never eaten anything common or unclean" (Acts 10:14). Robertson explains the key word: koinoi, "common," meaning what is "unclean either ceremonially or in reality" — the very scruple that "disturbed Peter on the housetop in Joppa" (Acts 10:14; cf. Hebrews 9:13). "Moral uncleanness is what makes a man common," he writes; the teaching "virtually abrogates the Levitical distinctions," and "one can see the pettifogging pretenders shrivel up under these withering words" (quoting Bruce).

Barnes states the doctrine without qualification: "the man, the moral agent, the soul, could not be polluted by anything that was eaten. What proceeds from the man himself, from his heart, would defile him." Clarke ties it to the actual charge: if "in eating with unwashed hands, any particles of dust... might happen to be taken into the mouth," this "did not constitute a man a sinner"; what defiles is "a corrupt unregenerate heart — a perverse will and impure passions." Gill voices the same against "the notions of the Jews," and Lightfoot glosses "defileth" as "maketh him common," noting that "they esteemed defiled men for common and vulgar men."

The witnesses present no real debate here, only a shared astonishment at the principle's scope. The schools agree that Christ is doing far more than settling a quarrel about handwashing: he is relocating purity from the body to the soul, from ritual to morality — a relocation so radical that the Levitical food-code itself would, in due time, fall away, and Peter would need a vision to believe it.
12:
"Knowest thou that the Pharisees were offended?" The witnesses note both the disciples' anxiety and Christ's refusal to soothe it. The Latin fathers labour over the saying it provoked — "Every plant which my heavenly Father hath not planted shall be rooted up" (v.13) — chiefly to refute the heretics. Jerome offers the careful theology: God's planting "none may root up," yet "since that planting was through the disposition of the will of him which was planted, none other can root it up unless its own will consents thereto." Chrysostom turns the verse against the Manichaean Manichaeus, who claimed it was spoken "of the Law": impossible, "for if He had said this of the Law, how would He have above contended for the Law?" Hilary identifies the unplanted plant as "that tradition of men under cover of which the Law had been transgressed." Remigius and the Gloss apply it to "every false doctrine and superstitious observance," and to "the doctors of the Law with their followers, who had not Christ for their foundation"; Raban explains they are "blind, leaders of the blind."

The grammatical-historical voices read the offence soberly. Robertson parses eskandalisthēsan — "were caused to stumble," "took umbrage at the public rebuke and at such a scorpion sting... It cut to the quick because it was true." Ellicott senses the disciples "half perplexed at the teaching itself, half afraid of what might be its immediate consequences," asking whether their Master "had calculated the consequences of thus attacking... the fundamental principle" of the party. Barnes notes the Pharisees "were so zealous of their traditions that they could not endure that their absurdities should be exposed," and Clarke observes drily that "none [are] so liable to take offense as formalists and hypocrites, when you attempt to take away the false props."

Gill records that the disciples came "full of concern" and "not so well understanding it themselves." The witnesses thus hold together two notes: Christ would not blunt the truth to spare the proud, yet the disciples' tender worry over the offended is treated gently — a foil to the obstinacy of the men they feared.
13:
"Every plant, which my heavenly Father hath not planted, shall be rooted up." The witnesses divide the verse between a warning against false systems and a deeper question about God's electing planting. The grammatical-historical voices keep it concrete. Barnes: "all those doctrines, of which his Father was not the Author, must be rooted up, or corrected" — the false doctrines of the Pharisees "must be attacked," and though "they were enraged... it could not be helped." Ellicott connects it to the parable of the tares just heard (Matthew 13:37-38): the Pharisees, "so far as they were a sect or party, His Father had not planted them," and "that doom is written on every system, party, sect which originates in man's zeal, in narrowness, in self-will." Clarke renders phyteia as "plantation," reading the whole Pharisaic body as "a plantation of trees, which God did not plant," doomed to be uprooted by "the Roman armies," with the Christian Church planted in its place.

The Alexandrian Origen again wields the verse against the dualists "who say that the God of the law and the God of the Gospel... is not the same"; he insists the heavenly Father of Jesus is indeed the husbandman, and yet "the Pharisees... were not a plant which His heavenly Father had planted," because of "their perverse interpretation of the things in the law."

The Reformed Calvin presses past the systems to the persons and to election. He rejects the notion that "all the inventions of men... must be rooted up," holding rather that "it was to men that Christ referred": "the reprobate must perish," for "salvation depends solely on the election of God." The planted are "those who, by his free adoption, have been ingrafted into the tree of life" (Isaiah 60:21); "no man will remain steadfast, unless his salvation be secured by the election of God." Wesley, characteristically, keeps it doctrinal and brief — "Every plant — that is, every doctrine." The debate is real: the grammarians and fathers read the plant as false teaching and its teachers; Calvin reads it as the reprobate man whose rooting-up displays God's sovereign planting. Both sides agree that what is not of God will not stand.
14:
"Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind. And if the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch." The witnesses converge on the proverb's force while debating the meaning of "let them alone." Gill reads it pastorally: "do not mind their anger and resentment... if they will go, let them go; they are a worthless generation of men." Barnes balances it: "do not be troubled at it... Be not anxious about it" — yet "he does not mean to suffer them to remain in error, without any attempt to refute or correct them — for this he was doing then." The point is not to ignore false teachers but not to be ruled by their displeasure.

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) presses the harder reading and turns it into Reformation polemic: "These words have been sadly misunderstood." They do not mean that "blind and deceitful teachers should not be pointed out to the people"; the sense is "give them up, have no kind of religious connection with them." The Codex Bezae even reads "give up these blind men." "A literal attention to these words of our Lord produced the Reformation." He drives the proverb home: "if the minister be ignorant, he cannot teach what he does not know... both shall fall into the pit of perdition together," for the people "should have searched the Scriptures."

The grammatical-historical voices supply the background. Ellicott notes from Romans 2:19 that "blind leaders of the blind" inverts the Rabbi's own ideal of guiding the blind, and that the proverb, "as now spoken, had the character of a prophecy" — the very "ditch" toward which teachers and people hurried in the years before Jerusalem's fall. Robertson keeps it vivid and unafraid: "Jesus is not afraid of the Pharisees. Let them alone to do their worst. Blind leaders and blind victims will land in the ditch."

The Reformed Calvin draws the famous rule of offences: "we ought to beware of offending the weak, but if any obstinate and malicious person take offense, we ought not to be uneasy," lest, to please the obstinate, "we must bury Christ." Whoever follows blind guides "will miserably perish; for when they stumble on a plain road, it is evident that they are willfully blind." The witnesses agree: the doom is mutual, the followers as guilty as the guides, for both refused the light.
15:
Peter asks, "Declare unto us this parable," and the witnesses note both the request and Christ's gentle rebuke of it. The Latin fathers read it as a lesson in interpretation. Remigius observes that since the Lord "was used to speak in parables," Peter "thought it was spoken as a parable"; and because he asked "on behalf of the rest, they are all included in the rebuke." Jerome draws the hermeneutical maxim: "the hearer is to be blamed who would take dark sayings as clear, or clear sayings as obscure." He then defends Christ's physiology against cavillers and, more importantly, locates the seat of the soul "not according to Plato in the brain, but according to Christ in the heart" — refuting "those who think that evil thoughts are suggestions of the Devil, and do not spring from our proper will." The devil "may encourage and abet evil thoughts, but not originate them." Augustine elaborates the body's two "mouths," one of flesh and one of the heart, and the Gloss lists the sins about to be named against the commandments of the Decalogue.

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the request as betraying offence rather than mere ignorance: "the disciples were offended at it," and Peter, "who is ever zealous, is not silent even so." Christ answers "after Jewish infirmity," conceding that food "goes out" — though even if it abode it would not defile — because "they could not yet hear these things."

The grammatical-historical voices keep the focus on what Peter actually meant. Robertson insists the parable to be explained is "in verse 11, not in verse 14" — the disciples "had been upset by Christ's powerful exposure of the Corban duplicity and the words about defilement." Ellicott agrees: "the strange, startling utterance of Matthew 15:11" seemed "a dark enigmatic saying, which required an explanation, like that... of the parable of the Sower." Barnes notes that "to the disciples, just coming out of Judaism... the doctrine of Jesus was obscure," and harmonizes Matthew's "Peter" with Mark's "his disciples" — "an omission is not a contradiction." Gill pictures Peter as their forward spokesman. The schools agree: the difficulty lay not in Christ's words but in the disciples' Pharisaic upbringing, which made a plain truth seem a riddle.
16:
"Are ye also yet without understanding?" The witnesses hear in the question a loving but real reproof, and they parse its sharpness with care. Gill reads it as Christ "wondering at, and... displeased with, and... reproving them for their dulness and ignorance." Barnes frames it as an appeal "to their common sense": Christ "wonders that they had not yet learned to judge the foolish traditions of the Jews by the decisions of common sense, and by his own instructions."

The grammatical-historical voices add precision to the wording. Clarke argues that akmēn, which the Authorized Version renders "yet," "should be here rendered still: Are ye still void of understanding?" — citing Greek usage. Robertson agrees that akmēn is "an adverbial accusative... at this point of time, just now," a form "condemned by the old grammarians" but found "in papyri and inscriptions"; and he supplies the heart of the rebuke: "In spite of all my teaching, are ye also like the Pharisees without spiritual insight and grasp?" His pastoral observation grounds the whole: "One must never forget that the disciples lived in a Pharisaic environment. Their religious world-outlook was Pharisaic" — hence their slowness was the slowness of men whose every assumption had to be unlearned.

Ellicott makes the emphasis explicit: the pronoun is stressed — "Ye, My disciples, who have heard from My lips the spiritual nature of My kingdom, are ye too, like the Pharisees, still such backward scholars?" The implied contrast is the sting: it is one thing for the Pharisees, who "know nothing of the Messiah's kingdom," to misunderstand; quite another for those who had "embraced it... and preached it to others" (as Matthew Henry put it under v.10).

The thin but pointed comment here reflects the verse's brevity. The schools do not divide; they simply register that Christ holds his own disciples to a higher standard than the crowd, expecting "some proportion of knowledge, and grace, and wisdom, according to the time and means we have had." The rebuke is the rebuke of a teacher who loves his pupils enough to be disappointed in them, and who will, in the next verses, patiently explain what they should already have grasped.
17:
"Whatsoever entereth in at the mouth goeth into the belly, and is cast out into the draught." The witnesses, almost wholly grammatical-historical, treat the verse as Christ's plain physiological argument that food cannot touch the soul. Barnes states it directly: "The food which is eaten does not affect the mind, and therefore cannot pollute it." The Pharisaic doctrine "that neglect of washing... defiles a man, cannot be true," for these "pertain to the body as much as food does, and they cannot affect the soul... The seat of corruption is within."

The grammarians dwell on the blunt word "draught." Clarke labours over aphedrōna, surveying Diodati's "nella latrina" and a Persian paraphrase, concluding it means the place where "what is not fit for nourishment is evacuated." Ellicott confirms it "is used in its old English meaning, as equivalent to drain, sewer, cesspool" (2 Kings 10:27), and weighs Mark's added phrase "purging all meats" (Mark 7:19): "a process purely physical from first to last cannot in itself bring any moral defilement." He guards the point — the appetites "connected with that process might bring the taint of moral evil; but then these appetites... took their place among the things that came out of the heart, and not into it." Robertson lifts it to a maxim on the mind: "Christ expects us to make use of our nous, intellect... Intellectual laziness or flabbiness is no credit to a devout soul."

Wesley adds a wry word on the evangelists' honesty — "Never concealing or excusing their own blemishes" — that the disciples' dulness is itself recorded. Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern) supplies the one note that lifts the verse above digestion: catechizing on the Lord's Prayer, he contrasts common bread, which "goeth not into the belly and is cast out into the draught," with "this Holy Bread" of the Eucharist, which "is distributed into thy whole system for the benefit of body and soul." The schools agree on the literal sense — food passes through and cannot defile the soul — while Cyril alone turns the very contrast to sacramental use, marking the supernatural Bread that does what natural bread cannot.
18:
"But those things which proceed out of the mouth come forth from the heart, and they defile the man." The witnesses identify the heart as the true fountain of defilement, and the catalogue of sins as its outflow. The Alexandrian Origen gives the spiritual anatomy: the evils "do not defile the man because of their proceeding out of the mouth, but have the cause of their defilement in the heart"; "the spring and source... of every sin are evil thoughts; for, unless these gained the mastery, neither murders nor adulteries... would exist." His pastoral conclusion follows: "each man must keep his own heart with all watchfulness," for the Lord "will bring to light the hidden things of darkness."

Barnes (grammatical-historical) supplies the fullest enumeration, walking through Mark's longer list and tying each sin to its commandment: evil thoughts are "the first things... the fountains of all others"; murders have "malice... seated in the heart"; thefts violate "two commandments — the tenth in thought, and the eighth in act"; false witness "proceeds from a desire to injure others"; blasphemies from "opposition to God." He adds Mark's covetousness, wickedness, deceit, lasciviousness, the evil eye, pride, and "foolishness" — "not want of intellect... but moral folly... All sin is folly." His exclamation captures the school's verdict: "What an array of crimes to proceed from the heart of man! What a proof of guilt!... How universal is depravity!"

Robertson sharpens the definition: "Spoken words come out of the heart and so are a true index of character. By 'heart' Jesus means not just the emotional nature, but the entire man, the inward life of evil thoughts that issue in words and deeds." Pointedly, "the captious quibblings of the Pharisees, for instance, had come out of evil hearts."

Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern) adds the moral theology of the members: from Adam onward "the members are not the cause of sin to those who use them, but the wicked mind"; the limbs "are provided as servants to thy reason," to be guided "by the motion of piety" and bridled "by the fear of God." Gill names the heart as the place where wickedness is "devised, and forged." The schools agree without dissent: defilement is moral, internal, and universal — the heart, not the hand or the food, is what must be cleansed.
19:
"For out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, false witness, blasphemies." The witnesses expound the list as the heart's inventory, and the grammatical-historical voices weigh its order and its words. Ellicott reads "the plural form" as pointing "to the manifold variety of the forms of guilt under each several head," and finds the order "in some measure an ascending one, beginning with the thoughts... which are the first suggestions of evil, and ending in the blasphemies... which... have God and not man for their object." He hears in the beginning and end an echo of the Pharisees' own course — "those evil surmises which had led [them]... to words which were blasphemy against the Son of Man, and came perilously near to the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 12:24).

Clarke probes the Greek and the textual variants: "in the heart of an unregenerate man, the principles and seeds of all sin are found," and "iniquity is always conceived in the heart before it be spoken or acted." He notes that some manuscripts read phthonoi, "envyings," for phonoi, "murders" — "Envy and murder are nearly allied: the former has often led to the latter" — and defines blasphemy, when "applied to God," as "to speak impiously of his nature, works." Wesley keeps it terse: "First evil thoughts — then murders — and the rest," and explains that "railings" in the Greek "includes all reviling, backbiting, and evil speaking."

Gill anchors the doctrine: "All wicked imaginations, carnal reasonings, lustful desires, and malicious contrivances, are here included; which take their rise from... the corrupt heart of man."

The Reformed Calvin guards the figure of speech: to say sin "proceeds from the heart" is "not strictly accurate, but... involves nothing absurd," for the heart "pollutes the eyes by making them the ministers... of wicked desires"; Christ shows "that the heart of man is the seat and the source of all evils," its "proofs and results" appearing "in the sins themselves." Cyril of Jerusalem returns to Adam: "because of his disobedience he lost also the good things he had received," yet "the members are not the cause of sin... but the wicked mind." The schools speak with one voice: the catalogue is not a list of outward acts only but the visible fruit of an inward corruption that every commandment is broken to satisfy.
20:
"These are the things which defile a man: but to eat with unwashen hands defileth not a man." The witnesses read the closing summary as Christ's deliberate return to the exact point of the quarrel, and they are unanimous in its force though brief in comment. Gill states the doctrine: these sins "are filthy in themselves, and must pollute all in whom they are; they bring a defilement on the whole man, both body and soul, fasten guilt upon him, and expose him to everlasting punishment." Clarke restates the argument cleanly: "What goes into the mouth descends into the stomach... and part is ejected... Nothing of this kind defiles the soul, because it does not enter into it; but the evil principles that are in it... defile the soul, because they have their seat and operation in it."

Lightfoot (grammatical-historical) presses the precise word Christ uses: "He saith not with unclean hands, but unwashen; because... they were bound to wash, although they were not conscious that their hands were unclean." He cites the Talmudic tract Challah, where unwashed hands are called "impure" "merely because not washed" — exposing the artificiality of the whole scruple.

Calvin (reformed) explains the loaded Hebrew idiom behind "defileth": the Greek koinoi, "common," renders the Hebrew sense by which, "since God had set apart the Jews on the condition that they should separate themselves from all the pollutions of the Gentiles, everything that was inconsistent with this holiness was called common" — that is, unclean. The very vocabulary thus testifies that the distinction was ceremonial, bound to Israel's separation, not a permanent law of the soul.

Matthew Henry, who had carried the whole pericope under v.10, drew the conclusion the others assume: Christ "doth not yet repeal the law of the distinction of meats (that was not done till Acts 10) but the tradition of the elders which was tacked to that law"; therefore "to eat with unwashen hands... this defileth not a man. If he wash, he is not the better before God; if he wash not, he is not the worse." The schools close the section together: the contest that began over a basin of water ends with the soul's true defilement named, and the basin shown to be powerless to touch it.
21:
Jesus withdraws to the coasts of Tyre and Sidon, and the cloud of witnesses reads the journey on two levels — historical motive and the dawning of Gentile mercy. The grammatical-historical voices give the occasion. Robertson and Origen note he "withdrew" as he had before, avoiding the offended Pharisees and "waiting for the time for His suffering." Ellicott marks it "the one recorded exception to that self-imposed law of His ministry which kept Him within the limits of the land of Israel," comparing it to "the vision of Peter at Joppa" and "his entry into the house of Cornelius": Christ was "showing in act, as before in word (Matthew 11:21), that He regarded Tyre and Sidon as standing on the same level as Chorazin and Bethsaida." Clarke cautiously holds Christ went only "towards the coasts," remaining "the minister of the circumcision" (Romans 15:8).

The Antiochene Chrysostom answers the obvious objection — that Christ had told the Twelve "Go not into the way of the Gentiles" — twice: "that precept... was not obligatory on Him," and "He went not to preach," for Mark says "He even hid Himself." The Latin and Eastern fathers read the scene allegorically and morally. Jerome praises the woman's faith, patience, and humility — "she compares herself not to the dogs, but to the whelps" — and takes her daughter for "the souls of believers... sorely vexed by a daemon." Remigius and Augustine make her "the Holy Church gathered out of the Gentiles," and find in her cry "Have mercy on me" a pattern for sponsors who bring infants to baptism, so that "by the faith of Catholics... their sins might be forgiven to infants." Hilary calls her "the proselytes"; Raban, the despised Gentiles who "seek out in Scripture... the spiritual sense."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the whole story as "a gleam of that light which was to lighten the Gentiles" (Luke 2:32). Christ "went to look after this poor woman, whom he had mercy in reserve for," as "Elias to Sarepta" (Luke 4:26). Her address — "Have mercy on me, O Lord, thou Son of David" — "owns him to be the Messiah," and though "a Gentile, she owns the promise made to the fathers." The Reformed Calvin states the doctrinal point: "the grace of Christ began to flow to the Gentiles," an "early manifestation of the common mercy which was at length offered indiscriminately." Gill identifies the woman as a Phoenician, a Gentile. The schools agree: a step beyond Israel's border becomes the first foretaste of the Gospel's reach to the nations.
22:
"A woman of Canaan... cried unto him, saying, Have mercy on me, O Lord, thou Son of David." The witnesses dwell on her identity and on the faith her title reveals. The grammatical-historical voices labour to reconcile Matthew's "Canaanite" with Mark's "Syrophenician, a Greek." Gill, Lightfoot, Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson all explain that the Phoenicians "were descended from the Canaanites, the original inhabitants of Palestine," Semitic but pagan; "Canaan" and "Phoenicia" are used interchangeably in the Septuagint (Exodus 6:15). Ellicott confirms the term, in the later books, "used more specifically of Phœnicia and its inhabitants," and Mark's "Greek" means "a heathen." He adds, from Luke 6:17, a "more direct source of knowledge": people "from the sea-coast of Tyre and Sidon" had heard the Sermon on the Plain, so "the mother of the demoniac daughter may well have cherished for months the hope" of reaching the Deliverer.

The witnesses give close attention to the name she uses. Robertson notes "she made her daughter's case her own, 'badly demonized.'" Clarke turns her cry into a model of penitent prayer: "How proper is this prayer for a penitent!... short; humble; full of faith; fervent; modest; respectful; rational; relying only on the mercy of God; persevering." Barnes observes she "showed great earnestness," falling at his feet (Mark 7:25), and that "Son of David" is "an essential character of the true Messiah."

The Reformed Calvin makes her confession the theological key: "she had acquired some taste of piety; for, without some knowledge of the promises, she would not have called Christ the Son of David." Though "an alien," a report of "the promised redemption was extensively prevalent," and "the sweet savor of the promises reached the neighboring nations." Against Servetus, who abused this text to prove "that faith may exist without promises," Calvin insists "faith always springs from the word of God... always found in connection with some light of knowledge." Wesley agrees she "had some knowledge of the promised Messiah." Among the voices, the curiously placed Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here belongs to the tares parable, not to this verse. The schools agree: a Gentile, outside the covenant, names Christ by his covenant title — and that confession is the seed of the great faith Christ will soon commend.
23:
"But he answered her not a word." The witnesses face the hardest feature of the story — Christ's silence — and they read it largely as a trial of faith, though one voice ventures further. Gill takes the common line: "Not that he did not hear her, or that he despised either her person or petition... but to continue her importunity, and try her faith, and make it manifest." Barnes agrees: "This was done to test her faith... It was not unwillingness to aid her, or neglect of her... It was proper that the strength of her faith should be fully tried." Clarke reads it as Christ seeming "to take time to consider her request, and to give her the opportunity of exercising her faith." Wesley simply notes, "He sometimes tries our faith in like manner." Robertson turns to the disciples, who "greatly disliked this form of public attention, a strange woman crying after them... They disliked a sensation," and asks pointedly whether they wished her "sent away with her daughter healed or unhealed."

The most searching comment is Ellicott's, who lays out "two alternative views." The ancient and majority reading holds "that from the first He had purposed to comply... and spoke as He did only to test and manifest her faith." But Ellicott prefers "the truer and more reverential course" of seeing "in what passed, the prevailing power of prayer working on the sympathy of Christ, and leading Him to pass beyond the ordinary limits of His appointed work." On this view "even the silence is significant, and implies a conflict": "the tenderness which He felt towards this sufferer... forbade" a quick dismissal, while "the sense of the normal limitation of His work forbade the other," so "silence was the natural outcome of the equilibrium of these conflicting motives."

The Reformed Calvin meets the theological difficulty that faith must come by hearing (Romans 10:14): "though he withheld... the words of his mouth, yet he spoke within to the mind of the woman," and her prayer "arose out of the" word she had already learned. So "the design of Christ's silence was not to extinguish the woman's faith, but rather to whet her zeal and inflame her ardor." He warns against the Papist inference that the disciples' intercession proves saints "are allowed to plead for us." Thus the witnesses divide: most read the silence as a deliberate test, while Ellicott alone reads it as a real conflict in Christ's human sympathy — but all agree the silence drove the woman to deeper, not weaker, faith.
24:
"I am not sent but unto the lost sheep of the house of Israel." The witnesses read the saying as the principle of redemptive order, not a refusal of mercy. The grammatical-historical voices stress the priority of Israel. Barnes: "He came first to them... as their expected Messiah... His ministry was confined almost entirely to the Jews," though "afterwards it was preached to the Gentiles." Robertson sees Christ making "a test case of her request": "In a way she represented the problem of the Gentile world." Ellicott explains what had restrained him: "Those wandering sheep... were the appointed objects of His care. Were He to go beyond that limit in a single case, it might be followed by a thousand," and "becoming, as it were, before the time, the Apostle of the Gentiles, He would cease to draw to Himself the hearts of Israel." He distinguishes this case from the centurion's (Matthew 8:10): the centurion "had built a synagogue" and was "practically... a proselyte of the gate," while the woman "belonged... to the most scorned and hated of all heathen races."

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) reads it as the divine appointment of the hour — "By the Divine appointment, I am come to preach the Gospel to the Jews only" — and turns it into a rebuke of lazy ministers who "sit down in the labors of others" rather than seek the lost in "the wilderness of this world." Wesley keeps the timing in view: "Not primarily; not yet."

The Reformed Calvin grounds the limitation in the Father's commission, not in any narrowness of Christ's power: "the power of Christ was [not] always confined within so narrow limits," but "present circumstances rendered it necessary that he should begin with the Jews." The middle wall (Ephesians 2:14) "was not thrown down till after Christ's resurrection." He notes Christ calls Israel "the lost sheep" — the designation given "not on the elect only, but on all who were descended from the holy fathers," for the Redeemer "was promised indiscriminately to all" of them. Gill simply observes the answer was addressed to the disciples, "who knew how limited their commission was." The schools agree: the saying marks the appointed sequence of salvation — to the Jew first — and is precisely the obstacle the woman's faith is about to overleap.
25:
"Then came she and worshipped him, saying, Lord, help me." The brief verse draws brief comment, but the witnesses note the deepening of her faith and reverence. Gill traces the progression in her titles: "she had called Christ Lord, and the son of David before, but now she worships him as God." Undeterred by "another repulse," she "pushes on, through all discouragements; her faith grows stronger, and her importunity greater"; she "followed the disciples into the house" and renewed her plea. Barnes defines the act: "she bowed down to him, did him reverence," and calls "Lord, help me" "a proper cry for a poor sinner, who needs the help of the Lord Jesus." Clarke reads her prayer as a claim to share Israel's mercy: "Let me also share in the deliverance afforded to Israel." Ellicott observes "the word implies the act of prostrate homage," and pictures her as having "apparently stood apart during the conversation between the Prophet and His disciples," now coming "again, renewing her passionate entreaty."

The Reformed Calvin probes the source of her persistence. She might seem to "contend with some measure of obstinacy, as if she would extort something from Christ in spite of him," but "there is no reason to doubt that she was animated by the conviction which she entertained as to the kindness of the Messiah." Even when "Christ expressly declared that it did not belong to his office, she was not intimidated by that refusal." The reason is decisive for Calvin: "she adhered firmly to that previous sentiment of faith... and admitted nothing that was opposed to her hope." This, he says, "is the sure test of faith, that we do not suffer that general commencement of our salvation, which is founded on the word of God, to be in any way torn from us."

The thin comment matches the short verse, but the schools concur in its lesson. Matthew Henry, expounding the whole story under v.21, had named the same movement: "Instead of blaming Christ... she seems rather to suspect herself," and "when the answers of prayer are deferred, God is thereby teaching us to pray more, and pray better." Her short prayer — "Lord, help me" — is, Henry says, "a good prayer, if well put up." The grammatical and Reformed voices alike see in this prostration the turning of a refused suppliant into a true daughter of Abraham, whose faith refuses to let go.
26:
"It is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it to dogs." The witnesses confront Christ's sharpest word, and they labour to soften neither its edge nor its intent. The grammatical-historical voices supply the crucial lexical nuance. Ellicott notes the word for "dogs" is "diminutive in its form," pointing "not to the wild, unclean beasts that haunt the streets" but "to the tamer animals... bred in the house, and kept as pets." So the saying, "even taking this into account," has "a somewhat harsh sound," yet "did not go beyond the language with which the woman must have been familiar," being "probably but a common proverb, like our 'Charity begins at home.'" Robertson agrees the diminutive kynaria, "little dogs," is what "she took... and deftly turned to her own advantage." Clarke calls the Gentiles "the curs; such the Gentiles were reputed by the Jewish people," and marvels: "What terrible repulses! and yet she still perseveres!" Lightfoot documents the Jewish slur — "The nations of the world are likened to dogs" — and notes how "this ignominious name, like a stone cast at the heathen, at length fell upon their own heads" (Philippians 3:2; Revelation 22:15).

Barnes is most concerned to clear Christ of cruelty: the words were "an expression of the highest contempt" in Jewish mouths, but "our Saviour did not intend to justify or sanction the use of such terms." It was "a trial of her faith": "Are you willing to receive of a Jew... a favour? Are you willing to submit to these appellations...?" — "He regarded her with a different feeling."

The Reformed Calvin gives the doctrinal weight. The "children's bread" is "not the gifts of God of whatever description, but only those which were bestowed in a peculiar manner on Abraham and his posterity" — the covenant blessing "which was to be expected in Christ" and "dwelt exclusively in the family of Abraham." To lay it open "without distinction... was nothing short of setting aside the covenant of God." Yet, Calvin adds, this restriction held only "for that time when it was in Judea only that men called on God"; once the Gentiles were admitted, "the distinction was removed." His chief design "was to make trial of the woman's faith." The schools agree: the harsh proverb, soothed by the diminutive and aimed at testing rather than wounding, marks the covenant priority of Israel even as it opens the door, by the woman's reply, to all who will humbly seek the crumbs.
27:
"Truth, Lord: yet the dogs eat of the crumbs which fall from their masters' table." The witnesses unite in admiration of the reply that turned an apparent rebuff into a winning plea. The grammatical-historical voices catch its wit and its humility. Robertson: "She took no offence at the implication of being a Gentile dog. The rather she with quick wit took Christ's very word for little dogs and deftly turned it to her own advantage, for the little dogs eat of the crumbs that fall from the table of their masters." Clarke draws out the argument: "I do not desire what is provided for these highly favored children, only what they leave: a single exertion of thy almighty power... is all that I wish for; and this the highly favored Jews can well spare." Barnes paraphrases: "Let me be regarded as a dog... Yet grant one exertion of that almighty power... and heal the despised daughter of a despised heathen mother." Ellicott notes the connecting "for" gives it force — "Yet grant what I ask, for the dogs under the table..." — and that "she did not ask that the children might be deprived of any fragment... but, taking her place, contentedly, among the dogs, she could still claim Him as her Master." He cites a strikingly parallel Talmudic story of Jonathan son of Amram, who begged Rabbi Jehudah, "feed me as a dog is fed, who eats of the crumbs of the feast," and was heard.

The Reformed Calvin commends her balance: "she was not hurried along by a blind or thoughtless impulse to offer a flat contradiction." She does not "dispute with [the Jews] the honor of adoption," but "only asks that some crumbs — falling, as it were, accidentally — should come within the reach of" the Gentiles; "at no time... did God shut up his grace among the Jews in such a manner as not to bestow a small taste... on the Gentiles."

Gill sums her self-abasement: "She owns all that he had said to be true... that she was indeed but a dog, and unworthy of any favour." Matthew Henry (under v.21) gave the classic praise: "A lively, active faith will make that to be for us, which seems to be against us; will fetch meat out of the eater." The schools speak as one: by humbly accepting the lowest place she lays hold of the highest mercy, proving that faith finds encouragement even in what looks like discouragement.
28:
"O woman, great is thy faith: be it unto thee even as thou wilt." The witnesses gather to praise the faith Christ crowns, and to mark the daughter's instant cure. The grammatical-historical voices note the change the reply effected. Ellicott: "The answer of the woman changed the conditions of the problem, and therefore... changed the purpose which depended on them." As with the centurion, "our Lord found a faith greater than He had met with in Israel"; she "was, in St. Paul's words, a child of the faith, though not of the flesh, of Abraham" (Romans 4:16). He draws the wide lesson — wherever any soul is counted "unworthy even of the lowest of spiritual blessings," the thought "that the dogs under the table eat of the children's crumbs" may "bring the faith that changes despair into something not far short of the full assurance of hope." Barnes defines the commended grace: "her trust, confidence," including "the humility and perseverance manifested in pressing her suit"; Mark records that "going home, she found her well and composed" (Mark 7:30). Robertson: "Her great faith and her keen rejoinder won her case." Clarke turns it to the discouraged believer: "Persevering faith and prayer are next to omnipotent... Be not discouraged at a little delay: when thou art properly prepared to receive the blessing, then thou shalt have it."

The Antiochene Chrysostom (under v.21) had already drawn the lesson that the woman "had contributed not a little to her daughter's healing," so Christ said not "Let thy daughter be healed," but "Be it unto thee according to thy will" — words "like that word which said, Let there be a firmament" (Genesis 1:6), so effectual was her faith.

The Reformed Calvin analyses wherein her faith was great: "by the aid of nothing more than a feeble spark of doctrine, she not only recognized the actual office of Christ... but pursued her course steadily through formidable opposition," and "so tempered her confidence with humility, that... neither did she shut against her the fountain of the grace of Christ, by a sense of her own unworthiness." This commendation of "a woman who had been a heathen... condemns the ingratitude of that nation which boasted that it was consecrated to God." His closing doctrine: "faith will obtain anything from the Lord; for so highly does he value it, that he is always prepared to comply with our wishes, so far as it may be for our advantage." Wesley names it simply: "Thy reliance on the power and goodness of God." The schools rejoice together: a Gentile's persevering, humble faith wins from Christ both his highest praise and his full gift.
29:
Jesus returns to the Sea of Galilee, goes up "into a mountain, and sat down there," and the witnesses read both the geography and its symbolism. The grammatical-historical voices fix the route. Gill notes he came "through the midst of the coast of Decapolis" (Mark 7:31) to "those parts of Galilee which lay near the sea side." Ellicott traces the longer circuit — Mark's better text has him pass "through Sidon," "the one instance in which He visited a distinctly heathen city," returning "through some of the mountain passes of the Hermon range." Clarke and Robertson discuss "the mountain" with its article — Clarke suggesting "mount Tabor," Robertson that he "was sitting there... possibly to rest and to enjoy the view or more likely to teach." Jerome pauses on the Greek kyllous, which "is not a general term for a maimed person, but a peculiar species," like one "crippled in one hand," and adds the tender image that Christ "goes up into the mountain, that as a bird He may entice the tender nestlings to fly."

The Antiochene Chrysostom observes Christ's varying method — "sometimes the Lord goes about to heal the sick, sometimes He sits and waits for them to come" — and praises the crowds' faith "in two points especially, in that they went up the mountain, and in that they... cast themselves at Jesus' feet," no longer even touching his hem but attaining "to a loftier faith." Unlike his slowness with the Canaanite woman, "to these He administers healing immediately... that He might stop the mouths of the unbelieving Jews."

The Latin and Alexandrian fathers read the mountain spiritually. Raban: having "in the daughter of this Chananaean prefigured the salvation of the Gentiles," Christ "came into Judaea" — "when the fulness of the Gentiles shall have entered in, then shall all Israel be saved" (Romans 11:25) — and "sat down... to shew that rest is not to be sought but in heavenly things." The Gloss allegorizes the sick: "The dumb are they that do not praise God; the blind, they who do not understand the paths of life... the maimed, they that are crippled in their good works." Origen makes the mountain "that which is more commonly called the Church," to which come not the whole alone but those who bring their sick. Matthew Henry (puritan-evangelical) keeps it pastoral: Christ "sits down... not on a stately throne... but on a mountain; so mean and homely were his most solemn appearances," "as one waiting to be gracious." The schools agree: the mountain becomes a seat of free, abundant mercy, healing the nations' image as it had healed the Canaanite's daughter.
30:
"And great multitudes came unto him... and cast them down at Jesus' feet; and he healed them." The brief verse draws brief but vivid comment, and the witnesses are chiefly grammatical-historical here. Gill pictures the throng coming "from the adjacent places," "leading some, and carrying others," drawn by the fame of his miracles. Barnes simply anchors the cure in Scripture — "he healed them" (Psalms 103:3; Isaiah 35:5-6) — pointing to the prophetic signs of the Messianic age, when "the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped."

Clarke fastens, as Jerome did, on the word kyllous, "maimed," and presses it to its strongest sense: from Hippocrates and from Matthew 18:8 (where one enters life "without a limb"), he argues it means "those who had lost a limb." This drives him to a remarkable Christological argument: "What an astonishing manifestation of omnific and creative energy must the reproduction of a hand, foot, etc., be at the word or touch of Jesus!" Since "creation... can belong only to God," and cannot be delegated without making the creature "the omnipotent God," he concludes: "this is to me an unanswerable argument for the Divinity of our blessed Lord. Others may doubt; I can't help believing."

Robertson keeps the scene physical and immediate: "they cast them down at his feet" is "a very strong word, flung them down, not carelessly, but in haste, because so many were coming on the same errand" (quoting Vincent). "It was a great day for they glorified the God of Israel." Ellicott notes that here "we get a great aggregate of miracles, unrecorded in detail," distinct from the single healing of the deaf man Mark records (Mark 7:31-37), "working on the minds of the multitude, and leading them to repeated utterances of praise."

The thin comment matches the verse, but the schools draw a shared conclusion that vv.29-31 form together: where Christ had healed one Gentile daughter at a distance and after delay, he now heals "all" who are brought, freely and at once. The contrast is the point — the same compassion that strained at the border now overflows on the mountain, and the haste of the people flinging down their sick answers the readiness of the Healer who waits to receive them.
31:
"Insomuch that the multitude wondered, when they saw the dumb to speak... and they glorified the God of Israel." The shortest comment in the chapter, yet the witnesses gather its sense. Gill describes the astonishment of "the spectators, who, though they came in expectation of seeing miracles wrought, yet these were so much beyond what they could have imagined" — "glaring proofs and evidences of the wonderful power of God." Barnes is content to cross-reference the prophetic fulfilment already noted (v.29), the signs of the Messiah's coming. Clarke universalizes the wonder devotionally: "And well they might, when they had such proofs of the miraculous power and love of God before their eyes. Blessed be God! the same miracles are continued in their spiritual reference. All the disorders of the soul are still cured by the power of Jesus."

The witnesses lean on the wider exposition the larger passage received. Matthew Henry (under v.29) had drawn out the doxology: "Christ's works should be our wonder... Miracles, which are the matter of our wonder, must be the matter of our praise." He noted the pointed phrase "the God of Israel" — they glorified "his church's God, a God in covenant with his people, who hath sent the Messiah promised; and this is he" (Luke 1:68) — and that even "the standers-by glorified God," so that "God must be acknowledged with praise and thankfulness in the mercies of others as in our own." Ellicott (under v.30) had observed that the aggregate of miracles led the crowd "to repeated utterances of praise in the form of a doxology."

The thinness of comment here is itself faithful to the verse: the witnesses do not invent meaning the text does not bear, but mark the natural movement from wonder to worship. The schools agree on the one note the verse strikes — that the proper response to Christ's power is not mere amazement but the glorifying of the covenant God of Israel, whose promised salvation these healings announce. Significantly, the very crowd that the Pharisees of vv.1-9 had been taught to lead now glorifies God on its own, a quiet rebuke to the blind guides who, seeing the same works, "blasphemed" (as Henry notes from the parallel). Wonder rightly directed becomes praise; and in that praise, the multitude confesses what the scribes refused — that here is the work of the God of Israel.
32:
"I have compassion on the multitude... and I will not send them away fasting." The witnesses expound Christ's compassion and labour chiefly to distinguish this feeding from the earlier miracle of the five thousand. The Antiochene Chrysostom marks the differences of detail: here the crowd "had not dared to ask for food, but He that loveth man... gives it to them unasked"; he waited till "the third day, when all was consumed that they might have brought with them," that they "might take the food... with keener appetite." He admires the apostles' candour — "though themselves are the writers, they do not conceal their own great faults" of forgetting the first miracle (v.33) — and notes that the differing number of baskets serves "that by this point of difference they might remember the two separate miracles."

The Latin and Alexandrian fathers read the scene typologically and harmonistically. Augustine uses it as his standard example against harmonistic panic: "if any of the Evangelists who had not given the miracle of the five loaves had related this of the seven, he would have been supposed to have contradicted the rest"; but since the same writers give both, "it is understood at once that they were two separate miracles." Jerome, Remigius, Hilary, Raban, and the Gloss spin out the numbers — the four thousand for "the four quarters of the earth" and the four cardinal virtues, the seven loaves for "the Scripture of the New Testament," the three days for the three ages "before the Law... under the Law... under grace." Remigius finds in the one scene "the work of His humanity, and of His divinity" — compassion showing "human frailty," the multiplication showing "the working of His divinity" — and so "overthrown is the error of Eutyches, who said that in Christ was one nature only."

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices keep the focus on the compassion and the lesson. Ellicott rebuts the critics who treat this as a doublet of the earlier feeding: "the notes of distinctness are too numerous," the numbers all differ, the very word for "basket" differs (spyris here, kophinos there), and "our Lord's words in Matthew 16:9-10 distinctly recognise the two miracles." Robertson agrees the hunger "moves him to compassion," and he is "unwilling to send them away hungry." Calvin draws the doctrine: "the power of God is not restricted to means... whether there be much or little," as Jonathan said, "there is no restraint to the Lord to save by many or by few" (1 Samuel 14:6). The "three days" means not total fasting but want of "ordinary food." The schools agree: divine compassion, not human request, sets the miracle in motion.
33:
"Whence should we have so much bread in the wilderness, as to fill so great a multitude?" The witnesses dwell on the disciples' startling forgetfulness, so soon after the feeding of the five thousand, and draw the lesson home. Gill states the fact plainly: "The former miracle of feeding five thousand men... being quite out of their thoughts, they reply" with helpless perplexity. The grammatical-historical voices probe how such forgetting was possible. Ellicott offers a careful threefold answer: the disciples "think of the Power as inherent, and therefore permanent. To them it might seem intermittent, a gift that came and went"; the gathering of the former fragments "seemed to imply that they were not to rely on the repetition of the wonder"; and three days' delay, with hunger near exhaustion, "might well have led [them] to think that the power was not to be exerted now." Above all, Christ's own question "must have appeared to them to exclude the thought that He was about to make use again of that reserve of power," so they answered "simply following in His footsteps."

Robertson is blunter: "It seems strange that they should so soon have forgotten the feeding of the five thousand, but they did... They forgot both of them" — for "soon Jesus will remind them of both these demonstrations of his power" (Matthew 16:9-10). He defends the historicity against those who "scout the idea of two miracles so similar," noting both are "narrated in detail by both Mark and Matthew and both are later mentioned by Jesus," and that the scene is "in the region of Decapolis." Clarke turns it to a maxim: "Human foresight, even in the followers of Christ, is very short. In a thousand instances, if we supply not its deficiency by faith, we shall be always embarrassed."

The Reformed Calvin makes the failure a mirror for every believer: the disciples "manifest excessive stupidity in not remembering... that earlier proof of the power and grace of Christ, which they might have applied to the case in hand." Yet "there is not a day on which a similar indifference does not steal upon us," and "we ought to be the more careful not to allow our minds to be drawn away from the contemplation of divine benefits, that the experience of the past may lead us to expect for the future the same assistance which God has already... bestowed upon us." Matthew Henry (under v.32) had said the same — "Forgetting former experiences leaves us under present doubts." The schools converge: the disciples' lapse is recorded not to shame them only but to warn us against the unbelief that forgets what God has already done.
34:
"How many loaves have ye? And they said, Seven, and a few little fishes." The verse is sparse, and the witnesses' comment is correspondingly brief, almost wholly grammatical-historical. Gill reads Christ's question as gentle pedagogy: "In a very mild and gentle manner, taking no notice of their stupidity, nor upbraiding them with their forgetfulness of the late miracle, and willing to exercise their patience, and try their faith, asks." The point is the contrast with the disciples' anxious "whence?" — Christ does not rebuke but redirects, turning their attention from what they lack to what they have.

Ellicott draws a quiet observation from the answer's resemblance to the earlier feeding: "The few barley loaves and dried fishes, this was all their store, as they went from village to village, or passed days and nights on the hills of Galilee." The detail is "interesting as showing what was the provision habitually made by the travelling company of preachers for the supply of their daily wants" — a window into the simple, hand-to-mouth life of Christ and the Twelve. Robertson notes the Greek diminutive again: "a few small fishes" (ichthydia), matching the "little dogs" and "little morsels" elsewhere in the chapter, a touch of the eyewitness's precision.

The thinness of comment is itself instructive, and the witnesses lean on the fuller exposition the surrounding verses received. Matthew Henry (under v.32) had drawn the lesson from this exchange: "When we cannot have what we would, we must make the best of what we have, and do good with it as far as it will go; we must not think so much of our wants as of our havings." Christ "would have it seen how little he had to work on, that his power might shine the brighter." The schools agree on the verse's modest but real point: before the miracle, Christ deliberately establishes the scantiness of the supply — seven loaves and a few small fish for a multitude — so that the abundance to follow can be seen for what it is, the work not of the larder but of his power. The question "How many loaves have ye?" is the question of a Master who will multiply, but who first makes his disciples reckon honestly with their poverty.
35:
"And he commanded the multitude to sit down on the ground." A single brief act, and the witnesses comment sparingly, the voices being grammatical-historical with one devotional note. Gill reads the command as the quiet assertion of Christ's authority and intent: "Not regarding the smallness of the provisions, nor any further consulting with his disciples; but knowing his own power to increase this food, and determining to feed the multitude before he dismissed them, in an authoritative way ordered them to sit down upon the ground in rows, that they might be the better seen, and served."
