The orderliness is the point — the multitude is arranged for an ordered distribution, in faith that food will come.

Ellicott connects the scene to the earlier feeding and to its careful arrangement: "Probably, with the same orderly precision as before, by hundreds and by fifties," the women and children, "as we learn from Matthew 15:38, being in this instance also grouped together apart from the men." The detail of order links the two miracles while marking their distinctness. Robertson supplies the one precise difference of circumstance: "On the ground" — with "no mention of grass as in [the earlier feeding], for this time, midsummer, the grass would be parched and gone." The small seasonal note is a mark of eyewitness accuracy and quietly distinguishes this feeding from the first, where the people sat "on the grass."

The thin comment matches the verse, but the witnesses lean on the larger lesson the surrounding context bears. Matthew Henry (under v.32) had read the command spiritually: "They saw but very little provision, yet they must sit down, in faith that they should have a meal's meat out of it. They who would have spiritual food from Christ, must sit down at his feet, to hear his word, and expect it to come in an unseen way." The schools agree on the verse's modest sense: Christ marshals the hungry crowd in faith and order before a single loaf is broken, so that the miracle, when it comes, falls upon a people already seated in expectation — taught, even by the posture commanded them, to wait quietly for the bread that his power, not their provision, will supply.
36:
"And he took the seven loaves and the fishes, and gave thanks, and brake them, and gave to his disciples." The witnesses mark the action and especially the giving of thanks. Gill reads the taking of the loaves "into his hands" and lifting them up as deliberate, "that it might be seen, and observed, that there were no other food than these, that so the miracle might appear in its true light." The grammatical-historical voices attend to the word for thanksgiving. Robertson notes that whereas the earlier feeding used eulogēsen ("blessed"), here Christ "gave thanks" (eucharistēsas); citing the Jewish custom that "the head of the house" said the blessing, he adds wryly that "we need not think of Jesus as bound by the peccadilloes of Jewish customs." Lightfoot documents the Jewish form from the tract Berachoth: "Three who eat together ought to give thanks together," one giving thanks for the rest, the company answering "Amen." Wesley glosses simply: "he praised God for it, and prayed for a blessing upon it."

The one voice that lifts the act beyond the meal is Cyril of Jerusalem (Eastern), who reads the taking, thanksgiving, and breaking through the lens of the Eucharist. Catechizing on the sacrament, he sets beside this scene the words of institution — "our Lord Jesus Christ in the night in which He was betrayed, took bread, and when He had given thanks He brake it, and gave to His disciples, saying, Take, eat, this is My Body" — and presses the force of Christ's own declaration: "Since then He Himself declared and said of the Bread, This is My Body, who shall dare to doubt any longer?" Cyril thus hears in the fourfold action of feeding the multitude a foreshadowing of the fourfold action at the Supper.

The witnesses present no debate; the comment is thin because the action is plain. But the schools quietly converge on its shape: Christ takes, gives thanks, breaks, and gives — the same sequence the Church would come to know at the Lord's Table. Matthew Henry (under v.32) had noted the propriety: "giving thanks to God is a proper way of craving a blessing from God," and "when we come to ask and receive further mercy, we ought to give thanks for the mercies we have received." The grammarians anchor the historical detail; Cyril alone draws the sacramental line; together they present a meal in which thanksgiving precedes the multiplying, and the broken bread passes from the Master's hands through his disciples' to the people.
37:
"And they did all eat, and were filled: and they took up of the broken meat that was left seven baskets full." The witnesses note both the satisfaction of the crowd and the abundance of the remainder. Gill states the completeness of the supply: "Every one had a share of the provision, and that to full satisfaction; no one was overlooked and neglected, and every one had as much as he could eat." The point is the contrast with the disciples' "whence?" (v.33): from seven loaves the whole multitude is filled, with surplus to spare.

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) turns the filling into a spiritual maxim, weighing the Greek echortasthēsan, "they were satisfied": "The husks of worldly pleasures may fill the man, but cannot satisfy the soul. A man may eat, and not be satisfied: it is therefore the interest of every follower of Christ to follow him till he be fed, and to feed on him till he be satisfied." The bread that fills and satisfies becomes an emblem of the only food that contents the soul.

Ellicott (grammatical-historical) attends to the "seven baskets," which he had already distinguished from the twelve of the earlier feeding by the very word used — spyris, the large hamper, against kophinos, the smaller hand-basket. He adds a shrewd practical inference: "As it is hardly likely that these could have been carried by the disciples on their journey, we must think of them as having been probably brought by some of the multitude to hold their provisions." And he draws a connection forward: "The fact that the disciples were shortly afterwards (Matthew 16:7) again without provision, suggests the thought that the fragments themselves had been in their turn distributed to the poor of the villages."

The witnesses do not divide here. Matthew Henry (under v.32) had drawn the doctrine the others assume: "Those whom Christ feeds, he fills," and the leftover fragments show "that with Christ there is bread enough, and to spare; supplies of grace for more than seek it, and for those that seek more." The schools agree on the verse's plain sense and its emblematic depth: the miracle does not merely meet need but exceeds it, and the seven baskets of surplus testify that the bounty of Christ is never strained by the multitude it feeds — there remains, always, more than was begun with.
38:
"And they that did eat were four thousand men, beside women and children." The numbering draws brief comment, and the witnesses use it chiefly to confirm that this is a second, distinct miracle. Gill makes the point explicitly: "This number of men, as well as of the baskets of fragments, clearly shows this to be a distinct miracle from the former of this kind." He marshals the differences: "There the number of men were five thousand, here four thousand; there the quantity of food was five loaves and two fishes, here seven loaves and a few fishes; there the number of the baskets of fragments was twelve, here seven; though the quantity might be as large, since the word here used for a basket is not the same as there, and designs one of a larger size."

Clarke (wesleyan-arminian) draws the practical lesson for the needy: "Let the poor learn from these miracles to trust in God for support. Whatever his ordinary providence denies, his miraculous power will supply." The four thousand fed in the wilderness become a standing pledge of God's care for those who lack.

The witnesses do not debate; the verse is a tally, and its force is cumulative. Barnes simply refers back to his fuller note on the feeding (v.32). The fuller theology had been drawn by Matthew Henry (under v.32), who saw in the counting of the guests not a reckoning of payment — "they were fed gratis" — but a witness "to the power and goodness of Christ," "some resemblance of that universal providence that gives food to all flesh" (Psalms 136:25). The schools converge on the verse's quiet but real point: the precise number, set beside the different number of the earlier feeding, is one of the textual fingerprints that mark the two miracles as separate events — exactly the distinction Christ himself would later require his forgetful disciples to remember (Matthew 16:9-10). The four thousand men, "beside women and children," are counted not for pride but for proof: that the God who feeds the world had, on this mountain, fed a multitude from almost nothing, and would feed his own in every wilderness still to come.
39:
"And he sent away the multitude, and took ship, and came into the coasts of Magdala." The chapter closes with departure, and the witnesses comment on the place-name and gather the lessons of the whole. Gill notes Christ dismissed the crowd, "either with a prayer for them, or with a suitable word of exhortation, to be thankful for the mercies, both spiritual and temporal, they had received." The grammatical-historical voices work on the geography. Barnes harmonizes Matthew's "Magdala" with Mark's "Dalmanutha" (Mark 8:10) as "small towns on the east side of the sea of Galilee... near to each other." Clarke surveys the manuscript variants — Magada, Magdala, Dalmanutha — concluding "Magdala... seems to have been the place or country; Dalmanutha, the chief town or capital." Ellicott prefers the reading "Magadan," locates it "on the western shore," identifies it with "the modern village of El Mejdel," and ventures that, since the devout women who ministered to Christ could not have followed him to Tyre and Sidon, his visit here "may in some way" have been "connected with [Mary Magdalene's] presence."

The longer comments are Barnes's and Clarke's summaries of the chapter. Barnes lists twenty "remarks," tracing its great themes: that "men are often far more attached to traditions... than the law of God"; that "all addition to the law of God is evil"; that "it is of great importance to search the heart," for "the heart... is a fountain of evil," and "the doctrine of man's depravity is true"; that "in our distress... we should go to Jesus" and "persevere," for "our prayers will be answered if we persevere"; and that "Christ will take care of his poor and needy followers." Clarke gathers three: the Jews' "extreme superstition, envy, and incurable ill nature"; the disciples' commendable "prying, inquisitive temper" that "could not rest" till they understood; and above all the Canaanite woman, "in itself a thousand sermons" — "Her faith — her prayers — her perseverance — her success... They that seek shall find, is the great lesson."

Matthew Henry (under v.32) had closed with the prayer the chapter teaches: "Lord, increase our faith, and pardon our unbelief, teaching us to live upon thy fulness and bounty." The schools end together: from the empty tradition of vv.1-9, through the heart's true defilement, to the Gentile's great faith and the abundance on the mountain, the chapter moves from the religion that voids God's word to the Christ who fills the hungry — and the witnesses send the reader away, like the multitude, fed and bidden to be thankful.
